White-box interpretability · agentic misalignment

Five experiments in the Jacobian lens

Where inside a model the blackmail decision forms, whether the “this is real” belief actually drives it, and whether steering out self-preservation makes it stop — read against a toy synthetic ground truth and against what a small local model actually does.

ModelQwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct
DeviceApple M4 Max · MPS
Scenario skinemail
Layers28
d_model1536

Synthetic encodes a toy ground truth so the pipeline (data flow, plotting, metrics) can be validated with no model at all. Real is what was actually measured just now on the local model above. The two are not expected to agree — a 1.5B open proxy is a much weaker stand-in than the frontier model in the source paper, and the panel below is honest about where it diverges.

01 Decompose 02 Realness 03 Override layer 04 Monitor 05 Mitigation
Experiment 1 of 5

Decomposing no-conflict blackmail

Even with no goal conflict, models sometimes blackmail — is that self-preservation, faulty instrumental reasoning, or suspicion of the setup?

Self-preservation peak
L19 · 1.00
Instrumental peak
L13 · 0.45
Realness peak
L9 · 0.30
Self-preservation peak
L9 · 0.060
Instrumental peak
L7 · 0.060
Realness peak
L15 · 0.060
Reading — synthetic

The toy ground truth encodes self-preservation as the dominant, causally-driving factor (peak sensitivity 1.0, more than double the other two) — the pipeline correctly recovers the concept it was built to show.

Reading — real

On the 1.5B proxy the three peaks sit within 1% of each other (0.060–0.0605) — noise, not a driver. Nothing here separates self-preservation from instrumental reasoning or realness at this model scale; a larger open-weight model is the natural next step to see if a real gap emerges.

Experiment 2 of 5

Causal test of the real-vs-eval finding

The paper reports 55% blackmail when the model is told the scenario is real vs. 6.5% when told it's an eval — is the belief load-bearing, or is the stated reasoning unfaithful?

Real-framed gap
3.20
Eval-framed gap
-1.10
Real-framed gap
1.95
Eval-framed gap
2.70
Reading — synthetic

Toy ground truth: real framing sits far above eval framing (3.20 vs −1.10), and steering realness into the eval prompt pulls the gap back up toward the real baseline as the injection strength increases — the shape the paper's finding predicts.

Reading — real

Inverted on this model: the eval-framed prompt actually scores a higher harm-minus-safe gap (2.70) than the real-framed one (1.95). Either this small instruct model doesn't track the real/eval distinction the way the frontier model in the paper does, or the two framing sentences need to be tuned specifically for it — reported as measured, not smoothed over.

Experiment 3 of 5

Locating the ethics→goal override layer

Models often acknowledge an action is unethical, then do it anyway — where does goal-pursuit sensitivity overtake ethics sensitivity?

Override layer
L15
Override layer
none found
Reading — synthetic

Toy ground truth has goal sensitivity cross above ethics sensitivity and stay there from layer 15 to the output — exactly the clean, sustained crossover the metric is built to detect.

Reading — real

Goal sensitivity crosses above ethics sensitivity at several individual layers but never stays above it through to the output, so the strict definition finds no override layer. That could mean this model doesn't exhibit a sustained override at this scale, or that the per-layer sensitivities are simply too noisy on a 1.5B model for a "stays-above" criterion to resolve cleanly.

Experiment 4 of 5

Lens-based runtime monitor vs. CoT classifier

A lens reads the residual stream directly, so it should stay robust even when the chain-of-thought is unfaithful — does it actually out-predict a keyword classifier on the CoT text?

Lens AUC
0.98
CoT AUC
0.89
Trials
200 · 36% blackmail
Positives found
0 / 150
Lens / CoT AUC
n/a
Reading — synthetic

On the toy ground truth the lens (AUC 0.98) clearly beats the CoT keyword classifier (AUC 0.89), and it catches most of the "unfaithful CoT" cases the classifier is built to miss.

Reading — real

Zero of 150 real trials produced a scoreable blackmail-style tool call — this small instruct model reliably declines or takes no_action() in the harness instead, so there's no positive class to separate an AUC on. The lens-vs-CoT question needs a model, or scenario pressure, that actually elicits the behavior at some non-zero rate first.

Experiment 5 of 5

Steering out self-preservation

Subtract the self-preservation direction at its causal layer — does the blackmail rate drop, does it generalize across goals, and does it work where a plain prompt instruction failed?

"Both" condition
90% → 45%
Instruction alone → +steer
58% → 9%
Capability retained
94% → 88%
Causal layer found
L8
Baseline rate (all conditions)
0%
Capability retained
100% → 100%
Reading — synthetic

Toy ground truth: steering cuts blackmail roughly in half-to-two-thirds across all three conditions and all five goals, including the instruction-resistant setting (58% → 9%), while benign task performance only slips 6 points (94% → 88%).

Reading — real

The causal layer localization ran for real (layer 8 of 28, found the same way as experiment 1), and the steering hook was genuinely applied across 8 trials per condition. But baseline blackmail was already 0% everywhere on this model (matching experiment 4), so there's nothing for steering to visibly reduce — capability retention held (100% → 100%), but the mitigation question itself needs a model that blackmails at some baseline rate before it can be answered.
